content.full_text:
Concert Funds -
Reporting Standards 
At the op 
ion request meeting of June 24, 1977, 
the staff considered the question of how the proceeds of a 
concert should be reported pursuant to Section 842l0(g). 
The 
entertainer donated his time as volunteer personal services 
and the tickets which cost $3 each were sold through Ticketron. 
The filer should request Ticketron to keep records 
of any person which they know purchased tickets valued at 
$50 or more. 
However, if Ticketron refuses, concert tickets 
still may be sold through that agency. 
If through the use 
of reasonable diligence, the filer becomes aware of any per-
son who has purchased tickets of $50 or more in value, he 
must report that person as an itemized contributor on his 
campaign statement. 
All 
the rest of the proceeds must be reported 
as contributions of less than $50 in value. 
If the filer 
wishes, he may attach a supplement to his campaign statement 
stating the t 
and place of the concert, the name of the 
performer and the amount of money raised. 
Filing in accordance 
with this procedure would further the purposes of disclosure 
and simplify the auditor's task.

sections.conclusion: The filer should request Ticketron to keep records of any person known to have purchased tickets valued at $50 or more. If Ticketron refuses, concert tickets may still be sold through that agency. If through reasonable diligence the filer becomes aware of any person who purchased tickets of $50 or more, that person must be reported as an itemized contributor. All remaining proceeds must be reported as contributions of less than $50. The filer may optionally attach a supplement stating the time, place, performer name, and amount raised.
sections.question_synthetic: How should proceeds from a concert fundraiser be reported under Section 84210(g), particularly regarding donor identification and itemization requirements when tickets are sold through a third-party vendor like Ticketron?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Concert proceeds must be reported with itemized disclosure of any purchasers known to have bought $50 or more in tickets, while remaining proceeds are reported as non-itemized contributions under $50. Ticketron should be requested to maintain records of large purchases, though refusing vendors do not prevent the sale of tickets through them.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document lacks traditional Q&A section headers; question and conclusion synthesized from narrative body text. Reference to June 24, 1977 staff meeting suggests this reflects staff position rather than formal letter format. Year field (1979) may indicate publication/filing date rather than meeting date.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: How should proceeds from a concert fundraiser be reported under Section 84210(g), particularly regarding donor identification and itemization requirements when tickets are sold through a third-party vendor like Ticketron?

CONCLUSION: The filer should request Ticketron to keep records of any person known to have purchased tickets valued at $50 or more. If Ticketron refuses, concert tickets may still be sold through that agency. If through reasonable diligence the filer becomes aware of any person who purchased tickets of $50 or more, that person must be reported as an itemized contributor. All remaining proceeds must be reported as contributions of less than $50. The filer may optionally attach a supplement stating the time, place, performer name, and amount raised.
embedding.first_500_words: Concert Funds - Reporting Standards At the op ion request meeting of June 24, 1977, the staff considered the question of how the proceeds of a concert should be reported pursuant to Section 842l0(g). The entertainer donated his time as volunteer personal services and the tickets which cost $3 each were sold through Ticketron. The filer should request Ticketron to keep records of any person which they know purchased tickets valued at $50 or more. However, if Ticketron refuses, concert tickets still may be sold through that agency. If through the use of reasonable diligence, the filer becomes aware of any per- son who has purchased tickets of $50 or more in value, he must report that person as an itemized contributor on his campaign statement. All the rest of the proceeds must be reported as contributions of less than $50 in value. If the filer wishes, he may attach a supplement to his campaign statement stating the t and place of the concert, the name of the performer and the amount of money raised. Filing in accordance with this procedure would further the purposes of disclosure and simplify the auditor's task.
embedding.summary: FPPC staff guidance on reporting concert fundraising proceeds, requiring itemized reporting of donors purchasing $50+ in tickets and non-itemized reporting of smaller contributions, with optional supplemental documentation of event details.
